rationale.AWR: PhD wellbeing surveys (2018) and an active social-safety/wellbeing program show structured suffering detection; not independently audited. Key signal: institutional PhD wellbeing survey + communities-of-practice on wellbeing.
rationale.EMP: Confidential complaints procedure and ombuds channels exist, but the Susanne Tauber social-safety/academic-freedom dismissal case became a national symbol of validation failure. Key signal: Tauber case + open letter.
rationale.ACT: Student/PhD counselling and confidential advisers exist with defined pathways, but no public wait-time or outcome data. Key signal: standard Dutch student-wellbeing provision, no published responsiveness metrics.
rationale.EQU: Dutch public funding model and diversity/inclusion office give broad access; tuition is regulated and low, but disaggregated outcome gaps not published in English. Key signal: regulated low tuition + D&I office.
rationale.BND: Dutch national LNVH/YAG reporting documents a sector pattern of protecting senior PIs over junior staff; the Tauber case is a concrete BND failure on academic-freedom boundaries. Key signal: junior-staff-bears-consequences pattern.
rationale.ACC: The institution contested rather than self-disclosed the Tauber harm, and the labour court partly faulted it; acknowledgment came under external pressure. Key signal: contested dismissal, external vindication of complainant concerns.
rationale.SYS: Codes of conduct on integrity, multi-year strategy, and Dutch national academic-culture reform engagement show some root-cause orientation. Key signal: integrity code + sector wellbeing-culture engagement.
rationale.INT: Stated social-safety values were undercut by the Tauber handling, exposing a values-behaviour gap acknowledged externally more than internally. Key signal: values-vs-conduct gap in social-safety case.

rationale.AWR: Healthy Campus Bonn program and advising/support network provide structured detection channels for students and doctoral researchers. Key signal: Healthy Campus Bonn + advice/support network.
rationale.EMP: Independent ombudsperson office for doctoral students, postdocs and supervisors provides a genuine mediation/validation channel. Key signal: independent ombuds office for early-career researchers.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and advising services exist with defined access points; German public-university norms but no public wait-time data. Key signal: standard student counselling provision, no responsiveness metrics published.
rationale.EQU: Strong, evidenced diversity strategy (5 focus areas), re-audited diversity certificate 2024, and met its female-professor target early; tuition-free public access. Key signal: met 30% female-professor target + audited diversity certificate.
rationale.BND: German Wissenschaftszeitvertragsgesetz drives fixed-term precarity for early-career staff sector-wide; no specific institutional mitigation evidenced. Key signal: German fixed-term-contract precarity, ombuds as partial counterweight.
rationale.ACC: Ombuds and integrity complaint procedures exist but no public record of self-disclosed institutional harm or transparency reporting in English. Key signal: complaint mechanisms present, transparency record thin.
rationale.SYS: Excellence Strategy investment and structural diversity-strategy targets show some long-horizon, root-cause orientation. Key signal: Excellence Strategy + multi-year diversity targets met.
rationale.INT: Met its stated equity targets ahead of schedule, indicating values-behaviour alignment on at least one measurable commitment. Key signal: delivered on stated female-professor commitment.

rationale.AWR: UK OfS-driven wellbeing reporting, counselling services, and the racism report documenting lived staff/student harm show structured, disaggregated detection. Key signal: published racism report with lived-experience evidence.
rationale.EMP: The racism report centred staff/student testimony of harm and the institution responded with an action plan, showing validation beyond legal minimum. Key signal: harm testimony treated as actionable evidence.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and disability services with national-benchmark context, but UK-sector counselling demand pressure and no published wait times cap responsiveness. Key signal: standard UK provision under demand pressure.
rationale.EQU: Strong widening-participation record under Scottish access duties plus the landmark slavery audit make equity a leading dimension. Key signal: Scottish widening-access duties + slavery audit findings.
rationale.BND: Endemic UK casualisation and zero-hours/fixed-term contracts (UCU 2023-24 dispute) constrain worker boundaries; no evidence Glasgow is an outlier upward. Key signal: UCU casualisation dispute, sector-typical precarity.
rationale.ACC: The slavery audit was a substantial self-initiated acknowledgment of historical harm with a 20-year reparative programme — a strong AB1/AB5 signal. Key signal: self-commissioned slavery audit + reparation programme.
rationale.SYS: The GBP 20m / 20-year reparative-justice programme with UWI and a dedicated masters address root structural harm, not just symptoms. Key signal: 20-year reparatory-justice partnership with UWI.
rationale.INT: Committing real money over two decades to reparations, with no near-term reputational necessity, evidences non-performative values alignment. Key signal: sustained financial commitment to reparations.

rationale.AWR: Behavioral medicine/SHS, wellbeing programming and CCMH participation give structured detection; grad-worker distress surfaced largely through the strike rather than early monitoring. Key signal: counselling infrastructure, but labor distress detected via conflict.
rationale.EMP: A national first-gen support model shows genuine resonance for some groups, but the 7-month strike and a class-action over late pay indicate workers felt processed, not heard. Key signal: first-gen care vs. adversarial grad-worker relations.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and emergency-aid services exist; the eventual contract delivered a large stipend raise, but only after the longest US higher-ed strike in a decade. Key signal: response came late and under sustained pressure.
rationale.EQU: Recognized national model for first-generation and Pell student success with documented programming and completion focus. Key signal: national first-gen / Pell success model.
rationale.BND: Seven-month grad-worker strike, unresolved ResLife (RA) bargaining, and a late-pay class action point to depletion and weak labor boundaries. Key signal: longest US higher-ed strike in a decade + late-pay suit.
rationale.ACC: Engaged in good-faith bargaining and reached a contract, but acknowledgment of grad-worker grievances followed prolonged conflict, not self-initiated repair. Key signal: resolution under pressure.
rationale.SYS: Sustained first-gen investment shows some root-cause access work, but no strong structural-advocacy record evidenced. Key signal: first-gen pipeline investment.
rationale.INT: Stated care-for-community values sat in tension with a record-length strike and late-payment litigation, exposing a values-behaviour gap toward workers. Key signal: strike duration vs. stated values.

rationale.AWR: Swiss psychosocial-safety scrutiny and UZH counselling/ombuds structures provide detection channels; no public disaggregated audit. Key signal: ombuds + psychosocial-safety sector attention.
rationale.EMP: Confidential support and mediation channels exist for doctoral students and staff in a sector with acknowledged power-imbalance vulnerability. Key signal: confidential support cells for PhD/staff.
rationale.ACT: Student and staff counselling provision is standard for a well-resourced Swiss university; no public wait-time/outcome data. Key signal: well-resourced but unmeasured responsiveness.
rationale.EQU: Switzerland's low-tuition public model gives strong access; equity disaggregation largely in German, limiting evidence. Key signal: low-tuition public access, thin English equity data.
rationale.BND: Swiss academia's documented PhD precarity and supervisor dependence cap worker boundaries; UZH has codes of conduct but the structural vulnerability persists. Key signal: PhD contract precarity + supervisor power.
rationale.ACC: Ombuds and conduct procedures exist; no strong English-language record of self-disclosed institutional harm or transparency reporting. Key signal: mechanisms present, disclosure record thin. NOTE: the Carollo astronomy-bullying case was at ETH Zurich, a different institution, and is not scored here.
rationale.SYS: Engagement with sector-wide psychosocial-safety and harassment reform shows some structural orientation. Key signal: participation in Swiss harassment-reform wave.
rationale.INT: Codes of conduct and zero-tolerance statements are in place; limited public evidence of costly values-tests passed or failed. Key signal: stated values, limited public stress-test evidence.

rationale.AWR: Annual Equal Opportunities Report, Swedish work-environment law (AML) duties, and national harassment surveys give structured, recurring detection. Key signal: annual equal-opportunities reporting + AML duties.
rationale.EMP: Student Health Service and student-union representation channels provide validation pathways; harassment still under-addressed per sector surveys. Key signal: student-union + health-service representation.
rationale.ACT: Swedish student-health provision and disciplinary/harassment-handling procedures give defined response, though wait times not published. Key signal: codified harassment-handling procedures.
rationale.EQU: Equal Opportunities Plan 2022-2027 with annual reporting, plus tuition-free access for EU students, makes equity a relative strength. Key signal: multi-year equal-opportunities plan + free EU tuition.
rationale.BND: Swedish collective-agreement protections and strong work-environment law give doctoral students/staff better boundaries than US/UK peers, though academic precarity remains. Key signal: collective-bargaining + AML protections.
rationale.ACC: Codified disciplinary and harassment procedures and integrity rules exist; limited public record of self-disclosed institutional harm. Key signal: procedural accountability, thin self-disclosure record.
rationale.SYS: Long-range equal-opportunities planning and engagement with national academic work-environment debates show root-cause orientation. Key signal: 2022-2027 planning horizon.
rationale.INT: Recurring public equal-opportunities reporting indicates values are operationalized, not only stated. Key signal: institutionalized annual values reporting.

rationale.AWR: CAPS, dean-of-students 'student of concern' pathways, and Title IX support structures give multiple detection channels. Key signal: CAPS + student-of-concern referral pathway.
rationale.EMP: Defined support structures exist but Purdue's high-pressure STEM culture and limited grad-worker voice (no recognized union) constrain felt resonance. Key signal: support structures vs. limited grad-worker voice.
rationale.ACT: CAPS and Title IX supportive measures provide standard response; no public wait-time/outcome benchmarking evidenced. Key signal: standard provision, no published responsiveness data.
rationale.EQU: A 13-year tuition freeze ($9,992 in-state) is a documented structural affordability commitment that materially lowers access barriers for lower-income students. Key signal: 13-year tuition freeze, ~$1B cumulative savings.
rationale.BND: No recognized graduate-worker contract in a right-to-work state; grad stipend/labor boundaries are weaker than unionized peers. Key signal: non-unionized grad labor in right-to-work Indiana.
rationale.ACC: Standard Title IX/OCR procedures and supportive-measures framework in place; no strong public self-disclosure or major resolution-agreement record found. Key signal: procedural compliance, no notable self-disclosed repair.
rationale.SYS: The sustained tuition freeze is an explicit, multi-year structural response to the affordability crisis driving student suffering. Key signal: tuition freeze as structural affordability strategy.
rationale.INT: Holding tuition flat for over a decade despite revenue pressure is a costly, consistent values commitment to affordability. Key signal: revenue cost borne for affordability.

rationale.AWR: UK OfS wellbeing duties and UBHeard/Spectrum Life monitoring exist, but in-house counselling was hollowed out, weakening proactive detection capacity. Key signal: UBHeard helpline, but depleted in-house service.
rationale.EMP: Outsourcing counselling to an external provider after chronic understaffing signals students are routed to a third party rather than institutionally held. Key signal: counselling outsourced to Spectrum Life.
rationale.ACT: Counselling capacity was reduced rather than scaled to need (posts unfilled since 2020), undermining proportional, timely response. Key signal: posts unreplaced despite documented need.
rationale.EQU: Strong UK widening-participation duties and access agreements give a solid equity floor. Key signal: OfS access-and-participation plan obligations.
rationale.BND: Work-related stress sick days among UCU staff rose from ~124 (2022) to ~913 (2024) and counselling staff were lost to severance — clear depletion and weak boundaries. Key signal: 7x rise in work-stress sickness absence.
rationale.ACC: UNISON/UCU publicized the counselling gaps; the institution's corrective action was outsourcing rather than restoring capacity. Key signal: union-surfaced harm, mitigation rather than repair.
rationale.SYS: Access-and-participation planning shows some structural orientation, but mental-health investment trend is downward, not upward. Key signal: declining in-house wellbeing investment.
rationale.INT: Cutting wellbeing staffing while publicly emphasizing student welfare reveals a values-behaviour gap. Key signal: welfare messaging vs. defunded counselling.

rationale.AWR: KTH self-published disaggregated figures from a national survey ('Double discrimination against women') showing it exceeds sector harassment levels — strong, honest detection. Key signal: published KTH-specific harassment data.
rationale.EMP: Despite good data, ~30% of female doctoral students reporting sexual innuendo indicates many do not feel safe or seen; reporting channels exist but resonance lags. Key signal: high female-PhD harassment exposure.
rationale.ACT: Anonymous reporting routes, an Equality Office, and a dedicated vice-president provide structured response capacity. Key signal: Equality Office + VP for gender equality.
rationale.EQU: Swedish free-tuition access plus a standing intersectional equality function; STEM gender imbalance remains a documented gap. Key signal: equality function vs. STEM gender gap.
rationale.BND: Swedish AML work-environment law and collective agreements give baseline protections, but the harassment data shows boundary enforcement is incomplete. Key signal: legal protections vs. documented harassment.
rationale.ACC: Publishing its own unflattering harassment figures and creating a VP role is a credible transparency-and-correction signal. Key signal: self-disclosed unflattering data + structural response.
rationale.SYS: Research-based proactive equality work at strategic and practical levels addresses root causes, not just incidents. Key signal: research-driven preventive equality program.
rationale.INT: Owning data that shows it underperforms the sector, rather than concealing it, evidences non-performative integrity. Key signal: disclosed reputationally-costly findings.

rationale.AWR: UC system counselling, CCMH participation, and basic-needs/early-alert programs give multiple structured detection channels. Key signal: UC basic-needs + counselling infrastructure.
rationale.EMP: Strong first-gen-serving culture (First-Gen Forward) creates genuine belonging for many, though the 2022/2024 UAW strikes show grad workers felt under-supported. Key signal: First-Gen Forward vs. grad-worker disputes.
rationale.ACT: Counselling, emergency aid, and basic-needs centers provide responsive services; UC-wide demand pressure limits wait times. Key signal: emergency-aid + basic-needs response capacity.
rationale.EQU: Among the very best US universities for access: ~49% first-gen bachelor's recipients, top-10 social mobility, #1 NYT College Access Index in California — a near-exemplary equity profile. Key signal: 49% first-gen + top access rankings.
rationale.BND: Participated in the largest US higher-ed strike (2022) and the 2024 UAW action; stipends rose but remain below high-cost-area living wage. Key signal: UAW strikes over stipend/cost-of-living.
rationale.ACC: Bargained contracts and operates Title IX/OCR processes; the 2024 encampment policing drew union grievances about contract violations. Key signal: contracts honored unevenly under protest policing.
rationale.SYS: Sustained access mission and Pell-recipient graduation focus address root causes of educational inequity at structural scale. Key signal: institutionalized social-mobility mission.
rationale.INT: Repeatedly choosing and sustaining a high-access, high-mobility model (vs. prestige selectivity) evidences durable values alignment. Key signal: consistent access-over-prestige posture.

rationale.AWR: Questionnaire studies surfaced that ~1 in 5 female doctoral students experienced bullying in the past year — evidence of active, disaggregated detection. Key signal: PhD bullying questionnaire findings.
rationale.EMP: Doctoral-student representatives, student unions, and Student Health Service provide voice channels, but the student-union report on department-head handling of discrimination shows validation gaps. Key signal: union report criticizing case-handling.
rationale.ACT: Equal Opportunities Plan 2024-2026, harassment guidelines, and Student Health Service give defined response pathways. Key signal: harassment-handling guidelines + health service.
rationale.EQU: Free EU tuition, a current equal-opportunities plan, and structured anti-discrimination duties provide a solid equity base. Key signal: equal-opportunities plan + free EU access.
rationale.BND: Swedish AML and collective agreements protect doctoral students relatively well, but documented bullying shows boundary enforcement gaps. Key signal: legal protection vs. documented PhD bullying.
rationale.ACC: Leadership acknowledged the bullying findings as serious, but a student-union report criticized inconsistent department-level handling of discrimination cases. Key signal: acknowledgment + criticized inconsistent handling.
rationale.SYS: Multi-year equal-opportunities planning and engagement with the national academic work-environment debate show root-cause orientation. Key signal: 2024-2026 planning + sector engagement.
rationale.INT: Surveying and acknowledging its own bullying problem reflects some non-performative honesty, tempered by uneven follow-through. Key signal: self-surveyed harm, uneven action.

rationale.AWR: As a health-sciences-only campus, UCSF runs trainee-wellbeing, GME wellness, and UC counselling structures with clinical-burnout awareness. Key signal: GME/graduate trainee wellness programs. SPECIALISED: no undergraduates.
rationale.EMP: Strong professional support culture, but the 2024 UAW strike and 2026 UPTE/CWA unionization drive indicate worker grievances about being heard. Key signal: ongoing unionization signaling unmet voice.
rationale.ACT: Well-resourced counselling and wellness services for grad students, postdocs, and residents; high-acuity clinical environment limits relief. Key signal: funded trainee wellness vs. clinical intensity.
rationale.EQU: UC access mission plus health-equity-focused mission; narrower population (graduate/professional only) limits undergraduate-access signal. Key signal: health-equity mission, graduate-only population.
rationale.BND: Participated in the 2024 UAW strike and faces a 2026 staff unionization push; intense clinical/research workloads strain worker boundaries. Key signal: strike + unionization over conditions.
rationale.ACC: Implements UC labor contracts and Title IX processes; the strike was partly over alleged contract violations during protest policing. Key signal: contract-compliance disputes.
rationale.SYS: Mission-level focus on health equity and research addresses upstream determinants of suffering. Key signal: health-equity research and care mission.
rationale.INT: UC public-mission and health-equity commitments are durable, though labor tensions test internal consistency. Key signal: mission durability vs. labor friction.

rationale.AWR: A small biomedical graduate/research institute with student-resource services; wellbeing detection exists but is not publicly disaggregated. Key signal: graduate-student support resources. SPECIALISED: biomedical research institute, no undergraduates.
rationale.EMP: Postdocs reported shared challenges over hundreds of organizing conversations, indicating prior support was insufficiently responsive to felt needs. Key signal: organizing testimony of shared unmet needs.
rationale.ACT: Generous stipends and a well-funded environment provide material support; counselling/wellness provision is present but lightly documented publicly. Key signal: well-resourced but lightly disclosed support.
rationale.EQU: Fully-funded PhD model removes cost barriers for admitted students, but the elite, small-cohort model limits broad access reach. Key signal: fully-funded PhD vs. narrow elite intake.
rationale.BND: September 2024 postdoc unionization (UPROR-UAW) signals that researchers sought stronger protections over conditions and dependence on PIs. Key signal: 2024 postdoc unionization vote.
rationale.ACC: No major self-disclosed institutional harm record found; standard HR/integrity policies. NOTE: the Tessier-Lavigne misconduct findings concern his later Stanford work, not Rockefeller, and are not scored here. Key signal: standard policies, limited disclosure record.
rationale.SYS: Mission focus on fundamental biomedical science is upstream by nature, though not framed as student/worker-suffering structural advocacy. Key signal: basic-science mission.
rationale.INT: Stable, well-endowed institution with consistent funding commitments to its researchers; limited public stress-test evidence. Key signal: funding consistency, thin public values-test record.

rationale.AWR: The Zero-Suicide-based 'Tech Ends Suicide Together' initiative with 30+ campus partners is an above-average, structured early-warning and detection system. Key signal: Zero Suicide community model.
rationale.EMP: Counseling Center free services and explicit reflection on whether 'culture of exceptionalism' harms students show genuine perspective-taking; resonance still strained by intense STEM pressure. Key signal: self-critique of toxic-excellence culture.
rationale.ACT: Counseling, after-hours support, and a coordinated suicide-prevention apparatus provide responsive crisis and ongoing care. Key signal: 24/7 crisis line + coordinated prevention.
rationale.EQU: Strong public-access mission and STEM-pipeline diversity work; affordability good but STEM gender/representation gaps remain. Key signal: public-access STEM mission.
rationale.BND: High-pressure workloads and limited grad-worker collective bargaining (Georgia restricts public-sector bargaining) cap worker boundaries. Key signal: intense workload + restricted bargaining rights.
rationale.ACC: The suicide-prevention overhaul followed documented student deaths and student advocacy — corrective, though reactive in origin. Key signal: reform after deaths + student pressure.
rationale.SYS: 'Tech Ends Suicide Together' explicitly targets institutional-culture root causes of mental-health harm, not just crisis response — a strong systemic signal. Key signal: culture-level root-cause mental-health strategy.
rationale.INT: Publicly examining how its own excellence culture harms students reflects honest, non-performative values work. Key signal: institutional self-critique published.

rationale.AWR: Counseling Center, OCRSM, and behavioral-intervention pathways provide structured detection; no public disaggregated wait-time audit found. Key signal: Counseling Center + OCRSM channels.
rationale.EMP: Graduate-assistant benefit policies exist, but Maryland public-sector grad workers historically lacked full collective-bargaining recognition, limiting voice and felt support. Key signal: GA benefits vs. constrained bargaining.
rationale.ACT: Counseling and GA health-care/tuition-remission benefits give defined response; responsiveness metrics not published. Key signal: GA benefits + counselling, unmeasured wait times.
rationale.EQU: Flagship public-access mission, Pell enrollment, and tuition-remission for graduate assistants support equity. Key signal: public-flagship access + GA tuition remission.
rationale.BND: Graduate-assistant stipend-vs-cost-of-living pressure near DC and limited bargaining power constrain worker boundaries. Key signal: high-cost-area stipend pressure.
rationale.ACC: OCRSM administers Title IX/civil-rights processes with defined procedures; no major self-disclosed institutional-harm record surfaced. Key signal: codified civil-rights office, standard disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Access mission and benefits structures provide some structural support; limited evidence of distinctive upstream advocacy. Key signal: access mission, modest structural advocacy.
rationale.INT: Stated welfare and equity values are operationalized in benefits policy; limited public evidence of costly values-tests. Key signal: benefits-policy alignment, thin stress-test record.

rationale.AWR: A dedicated social-safety organization, confidential advisers, and wellbeing structures give structured, named detection of distress. Key signal: institutional social-safety office.
rationale.EMP: Engaged seriously with student activists on fossil-fuel ties and is building an ethics framework for partnerships, indicating it takes stakeholder concerns as legitimate. Key signal: dialogue + ethics framework from student protest.
rationale.ACT: Dutch student-wellbeing and counselling provision with social-safety pathways; responsiveness data not public. Key signal: social-safety pathways, unmeasured wait times.
rationale.EQU: Regulated low tuition, broad public access, and inclusion structures provide a solid equity base. Key signal: regulated-tuition public access + inclusion structures.
rationale.BND: Dutch labor protections give relatively strong staff/PhD boundaries; the sector PI-protection pattern remains a watch-point. Key signal: strong Dutch labor protections.
rationale.ACC: Responded to sustained protest by tightening fossil-industry collaboration policy — a documented course correction under public pressure. Key signal: policy correction on fossil ties.
rationale.SYS: Tightening fossil-fuel partnership criteria and building a values-based partnership-ethics framework is a strong structural-critique and root-cause signal. Key signal: fossil-collaboration policy + partnership ethics framework.
rationale.INT: Moving institutional partnership policy toward stated sustainability values, at some cost to industry funding, evidences values alignment. Key signal: aligning partnerships with stated values.

rationale.AWR: The Cellule Confiance and harassment-support structures provide named detection channels in a sector under psychosocial-safety scrutiny. Key signal: Cellule Confiance external support cell.
rationale.EMP: External, free, confidential support for students, staff and supervisors signals genuine validation intent; PhD power-dependence remains a structural empathy barrier. Key signal: confidential tripartite support cell.
rationale.ACT: Student counselling and confidential harassment support give defined response; no public wait-time/outcome metrics. Key signal: harassment-support provision, unmeasured responsiveness.
rationale.EQU: Low-tuition Swiss public access supports equity; disaggregated outcome data largely in French limits evidence. Key signal: low-tuition access, thin English equity data.
rationale.BND: Documented Swiss PhD precarity and supervisor power-dependence cap worker boundaries despite support cells. Key signal: PhD precarity + supervisor dependence.
rationale.ACC: Post-#MeToo codes of conduct and confidential channels exist; limited public record of self-disclosed institutional harm or reparation. Key signal: reformed procedures, thin self-disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Participation in the Swiss harassment-reform movement and confidential-support infrastructure show some structural orientation. Key signal: sector harassment-reform engagement.
rationale.INT: Zero-tolerance commitments and support structures are in place; limited public evidence of costly values-tests. Key signal: stated zero-tolerance, limited stress-test evidence.

rationale.AWR: UM researchers actively study student depression prevalence (~30% in a 1,023-student study) and run mental-health symposiums, showing some detection effort. Key signal: institutional mental-health prevalence research. LOW CONFIDENCE: limited English disclosure of operational provision.
rationale.EMP: Counselling services exist, but the Malaysian legal framework (UUCA/AUKU) systematically constrains student speech and mobility, limiting genuine voice. Key signal: statutory limits on student autonomy.
rationale.ACT: Mental-health initiatives and counselling are present, but national reviews note effectiveness is under-evaluated and provision uneven. Key signal: initiatives present, efficacy under-evaluated.
rationale.EQU: Malaysia's public-university quota and access policies broaden participation, though the ethnic-quota system raises contested equity questions. Key signal: broad public access under contested quota policy.
rationale.BND: Statutory student-autonomy limits and constrained academic freedom (AUKU legacy) restrict consent and self-determination boundaries. Key signal: AUKU-era autonomy constraints.
rationale.ACC: Limited public, independent accountability disclosure; academic-freedom constraints reduce transparency. Key signal: thin independent accountability record (low evidence).
rationale.SYS: Engagement with national mental-health-policy reform via symposiums shows modest root-cause orientation. Key signal: mental-health policy convening.
rationale.INT: Operating within a restrictive statutory framework limits demonstrable independent values commitments; low evidence depth. Key signal: constrained autonomy limits values-test evidence. LOW CONFIDENCE.

rationale.AWR: A dedicated Resident Wellness and Counseling Center, GME wellness programming, and a 2024 student-mistreatment policy give structured detection for trainees. Key signal: RWAC + 2024 mistreatment-of-students policy. SPECIALISED: health-sciences-only, no undergraduates.
rationale.EMP: Confidential wellness counselors and an updated mistreatment policy show validation intent, but intense clinical/residency culture strains felt resonance. Key signal: confidential trainee counselling + mistreatment policy.
rationale.ACT: RWAC offers burnout and mental-health support tailored to residents/fellows with confidential access — a responsive, population-specific design. Key signal: residency-specific wellness counselling.
rationale.EQU: Health-sciences-only graduate/professional population narrows undergraduate-access signal; Texas anti-DEI legislation constrains equity programming. Key signal: graduate-only population under Texas DEI restrictions.
rationale.BND: Explicit anti-retaliation protections for Title IX reporters and a mistreatment policy improve boundaries, though residency workloads remain demanding. Key signal: codified anti-retaliation + mistreatment policy.
rationale.ACC: Strong written non-retaliation and grievance procedures (note: UTSW v. Nassar is the landmark retaliation case bearing its name) show procedural accountability. Key signal: codified anti-retaliation procedures.
rationale.SYS: Medical-center mission addresses health determinants; trainee-wellness reform is more symptom-level than structural advocacy. Key signal: clinical mission, trainee-focused reform.
rationale.INT: Updating its student-mistreatment policy in 2024 and codifying retaliation protections indicate operationalized values for trainees. Key signal: 2024 policy update operationalizing protections.

rationale.AWR: UPC co-runs a ~45,000-student longitudinal cohort on student precarity and psychological distress and offers psychological-support services — strong, research-grade detection. Key signal: 45k-student precarity/distress cohort.
rationale.EMP: Mental Health First Aid training and a Buddy System for international students show proactive, population-aware support; French student-precarity remains acute. Key signal: MHFA training + international-student buddy system.
rationale.ACT: Psychological-support services and student-health provision exist; national student-precarity data (~12% of health students precarious) shows demand outstrips provision. Key signal: support services vs. high unmet precarity.
rationale.EQU: Very low regulated French public tuition gives strong access; the merged Descartes/Diderot/IPGP institution serves a broad, diverse Paris population. Key signal: near-free public tuition + broad access.
rationale.BND: Acute documented student financial precarity (food insecurity, foodbank use) signals that consent-to-cost and self-sustainability boundaries are strained sector-wide. Key signal: documented student food insecurity.
rationale.ACC: Standard French public-university grievance and integrity structures; limited public self-disclosed institutional-harm record in English. Key signal: standard procedures, thin English disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Co-leading longitudinal precarity research and rolling out MHFA literacy programs addresses upstream causes of student suffering. Key signal: research-to-intervention on precarity.
rationale.INT: Investing in precarity research and peer-support infrastructure aligns action with welfare commitments; medium evidence depth. Key signal: research-backed welfare investment.
